content.problemStatement:
You are given a permutation PPP of length NNN. A permutation of length NNN is an array of length NNN that contains every integer from 111 to NNN.

It can be modified by performing the following operation:


Choose indices LLL and RRR (1‚â§L‚â§R‚â§N1 \leq L \leq R \leq N1‚â§L‚â§R‚â§N) such that the subarray [PL,PL+1,‚Ä¶,PR][P_L, P_{L+1}, \ldots, P_R][PL‚Äã,PL+1‚Äã,‚Ä¶,PR‚Äã] is **not** sorted in ascending order.
Then, do one of the following:

Move element PLP_LPL‚Äã to the back of PPP; **or**
Move element PRP_RPR‚Äã to the front of PPP.


After the move is made, the elements are re-indexed from 111 to NNN.
For example, if P=[2,4,1,3]P = [2, 4, 1, 3]P=[2,4,1,3], and you choose L=2L = 2L=2 and R=4R = 4R=4, your options are:


Move P2=4P_2 = 4P2‚Äã=4 to the back, to obtain [2,1,3,4][2, 1, 3, 4][2,1,3,4].
Move P4=3P_4 = 3P4‚Äã=3 to the front, to obtain [3,2,4,1][3, 2, 4, 1][3,2,4,1].
Define f(P)f(P)f(P) to be the minimum number of operations needed to sort PPP in ascending order.

It can be proved that it's always possible to sort any permutation PPP in ascending order using a finite number of these operations, so f(P)f(P)f(P) is well-defined.

You are given the integers N,K,N, K,N,K, and XXX.
Count the number of permutations PPP of length NNN for which:


f(P)=Kf(P) = Kf(P)=K, and
P1=XP_1 = XP1‚Äã=X
The answer can be large, so output it modulo 998244353998244353998244353.


Input Format

The first line of input will contain a single integer TTT, denoting the number of test cases.
The first and only line of each testcase will contain three space-separated integers NNN, KKK, and XXX.


Output Format
For each test case, output on a new line the answer: the number of permutations of length NNN for which f(P)=Kf(P) = Kf(P)=K and P1=XP_1 = XP1‚Äã=X, modulo 998244353998244353998244353.
constraints: The sum of NNN across all tests won't exceed 100100100.

sampleTestCases.explanation:
Explanation:
Test cases 111 and 222: For N=1N = 1N=1, the only permutation is [1][1][1] which has f(P)=0f(P) = 0f(P)=0 since it's already sorted.
Test case 333: The only valid permutation is [1,3,2][1, 3, 2][1,3,2], which can be sorted in 111 move by choosing the subarray [3,2][3, 2][3,2] and moving 333 to the end of PPP, obtaining [1,2,3][1, 2, 3][1,2,3].
